stem:
A city council notes that bicycle traffic rose after new lanes were painted on several streets. It concludes that repainting all old lanes will significantly reduce car congestion next year.

Which of the following is an assumption on which the council's conclusion depends?

choices.text: Bike use rises whenever lanes are repainted.
Most new cyclists previously drove cars on the same streets.
Congestion is caused only by cars, not by trucks.
The city can repaint lanes without changing traffic signals.
Car congestion has been falling for several years already.

distractorRationale.A: Restates the observed correlation as a general rule, but the conclusion is about reducing car congestion, not just raising bike use; the gap that must be bridged is whether new cyclists are former drivers.
distractorRationale.C: Whether trucks cause congestion is irrelevant; the argument needs the assumption that cyclists are coming out of cars, which is what B supplies.
distractorRationale.D: A logistical detail about signals; the conclusion about cutting car traffic depends on where the new cyclists come from, not on signal timing.
distractorRationale.E: A pre-existing downward trend would actually undermine the claim that repainting causes the reduction; the required assumption is that cyclists are former drivers (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. Most new cyclists previously drove cars on the same streets.

stem:
A bakery says that switching to a smaller oven will reduce monthly energy costs because the smaller oven consumes less power per hour.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the bakery's reasoning?

choices.text: The smaller oven consumes even less power per hour than the bakery had expected.
The bakery will need to run the smaller oven for many more hours to meet demand.
The smaller oven uses the same type of electricity as the current oven.
The smaller oven heats up more quickly than the current oven does.
The current oven is rarely run at its full capacity.

distractorRationale.A: Strengthens the bakery's case rather than weakening it; lower power per hour only helps. The weakener is that total hours rise, so total energy may not fall (B).
distractorRationale.C: Same type of electricity is irrelevant to total cost; the flaw is that per-hour savings can be erased by running the oven far longer (B).
distractorRationale.D: Faster heat-up slightly helps the bakery, not hurts it; the argument is undermined only by the increase in total run time (B).
distractorRationale.E: If the current oven is rarely run at full capacity, that does not show the smaller oven costs more; the weakener is the need for many more run hours (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. The bakery will need to run the smaller oven for many more hours to meet demand.

stem:
A hospital introduced a new triage checklist and found that average wait times in the emergency room fell during the next month.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the hospital's conclusion that the checklist caused the reduction?

choices.text: The emergency room was renovated at the same time.
The number of patients was higher than usual during the month.
Other nearby hospitals did not change their triage procedures during the month.
Some staff members preferred the old checklist.
The hospital also increased its parking capacity.

distractorRationale.A: A simultaneous renovation introduces an alternative cause, which weakens rather than strengthens the checklist claim. Ruling out other hospitals' changes is what supports causation (C).
distractorRationale.B: More patients than usual would make falling wait times harder, but it does not rule out other causes; eliminating the alternative explanation does (C).
distractorRationale.D: Staff preference for the old checklist is irrelevant to whether the new one caused shorter waits; the strengthener removes a rival cause (C).
distractorRationale.E: Parking capacity has nothing to do with in-ER wait times; the conclusion is strengthened by ruling out external triage changes (C).

explanation: The correct answer is C. Other nearby hospitals did not change their triage procedures during the month.

stem:
In a survey, nearly all students who attended the optional review session later passed the exam, but many students who skipped the session also passed.

Which of the following can be properly inferred?

choices.text: Students who attended the session were more likely to pass than those who skipped it.
Attending the session was the most effective way for a student to prepare for the exam.
Attending the session may have helped some students, but it was not necessary for passing.
Most students who passed the exam had attended the review session.
Attending the session was enough, by itself, to guarantee that a student would pass.

distractorRationale.A: Tempting, but the premise gives raw counts, not rates, so we cannot conclude attendees were more likely to pass; we can only infer the session was not necessary (C).
distractorRationale.B: Goes too far: nothing compares the session to other prep methods, so 'most effective' is unsupported. The valid inference is that it was not required (C).
distractorRationale.D: Cannot be inferred because many skippers also passed; the numbers do not establish that most passers attended. The safe inference is non-necessity (C).
distractorRationale.E: Overstates the evidence as a guarantee; near-certainty for attendees is not the same as sufficiency. The supported claim is only that the session was not necessary (C).

explanation: The correct answer is C. Attending the session may have helped some students, but it was not necessary for passing.

stem:
Some analysts argue that **higher minimum wages inevitably reduce employment**. Other economists reply that **labor markets may adjust through lower turnover and higher productivity**.

In the argument, the first boldface statement plays which of the following roles?

choices.text: A conclusion challenged by the second boldface statement.
An example used to support the second boldface statement.
A policy proposal endorsed by the second boldface statement.
A premise offered as evidence for the second boldface statement.
A definition introduced by the second boldface statement.

distractorRationale.B: The first statement is not an example feeding the second; the two are opposing claims, with the first being the conclusion the second rebuts (A).
distractorRationale.C: Neither statement is a policy proposal, and the second disputes rather than endorses the first; the first is a challenged conclusion (A).
distractorRationale.D: The first is a conclusion, not a premise supporting the second; in fact the second pushes back against it (A).
distractorRationale.E: The second statement is a rebuttal, not a definition; the first functions as the conclusion it challenges (A).

explanation: The correct answer is A. A conclusion challenged by the second boldface statement.

stem:
A city expanded its bus network, yet average bus travel time increased during the following year. City officials note that bus ridership also rose sharply in the same period.

Which of the following, if true, best resolves the apparent paradox?

choices.text: The city added more bus routes to neighborhoods that previously had none.
Several streets became bus-only lanes.
The new riders were concentrated on a few heavily congested routes.
Bus fares remained unchanged during the year.
Some riders preferred standing to sitting.

distractorRationale.A: Adding routes to underserved areas does not explain why average travel time rose; the resolution is that new riders crowded already-congested routes (C).
distractorRationale.B: Bus-only lanes would tend to speed buses up, deepening the paradox rather than resolving it; the answer is the concentration of riders on busy routes (C).
distractorRationale.D: Unchanged fares have no bearing on travel time; the paradox is resolved by where the surge in riders went (C).
distractorRationale.E: A seating preference does not affect travel time; the resolution is that new ridership piled onto congested routes (C).

explanation: The correct answer is C. The new riders were concentrated on a few heavily congested routes.

stem:
A software firm claims that because its newest product has the highest customer rating in the market, it will capture the largest share of sales next year.

Which of the following is an assumption required by the argument?

choices.text: Customers care more about ratings than about price, compatibility, and brand loyalty.
The firm spent less on advertising than its rivals.
The rating system was created by an independent firm.
The product was released after several competitors' products.
Customer ratings are updated every month.

distractorRationale.B: Lower ad spending does not help link top ratings to top sales, and could even hurt; the needed assumption is that ratings drive purchases over other factors (A).
distractorRationale.C: Who created the rating system does not bridge rating to market share; the argument assumes ratings outweigh price, compatibility, and loyalty (A).
distractorRationale.D: Release timing relative to rivals is irrelevant to the leap from rating to sales; the required assumption is A.
distractorRationale.E: How often ratings update does not connect a high rating to the largest sales share; the assumption is that ratings matter more than other buying factors (A).

explanation: The correct answer is A. Customers care more about ratings than about price, compatibility, and brand loyalty.

stem:
A town argues that building a new parking garage will revive its downtown businesses because more drivers will be able to park nearby.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: The garage will be built two blocks from the main shopping street.
Most downtown customers already arrive on foot or by public transit.
Parking rates in nearby lots are expected to rise.
The town has a seasonal festival every summer.
Several stores plan to renovate their facades.

distractorRationale.A: A two-block distance is a minor detail and does not undercut the core link between parking and revival as decisively as the fact that customers do not drive (B).
distractorRationale.C: Rising rates at other lots would make the new garage more attractive, strengthening the plan rather than weakening it; the weakener is that customers arrive on foot or transit (B).
distractorRationale.D: A summer festival is irrelevant to whether more parking revives business; the argument is undercut by customers not driving (B).
distractorRationale.E: Facade renovations would help downtown, not hurt the argument; the weakener shows parking is not the binding constraint (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. Most downtown customers already arrive on foot or by public transit.

stem:
A researcher found that students who slept at least seven hours before an exam scored higher on average than students who slept less.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the conclusion that sleep contributed to the higher scores?

choices.text: Students who slept more also studied roughly the same number of hours as students who slept less.
The exam was graded by a single instructor.
Students chose their own bedtime on the night before the exam.
The campus library was open late that night.
The exam covered material from two chapters.

distractorRationale.B: A single grader controls for grading bias, but the threat here is that sleepers also studied differently; the strengthener holds study time roughly equal (A).
distractorRationale.C: Letting students pick their own bedtime introduces self-selection, which weakens causal inference rather than strengthening it; equal study time is what supports it (A).
distractorRationale.D: Library hours do not isolate sleep as the cause; the conclusion is strengthened by ruling out a study-time difference (A).
distractorRationale.E: The number of chapters covered is irrelevant to whether sleep caused higher scores; the strengthener equalizes study effort (A).

explanation: The correct answer is A. Students who slept more also studied roughly the same number of hours as students who slept less.

stem:
A museum reported that attendance rose after it replaced paper labels with short audio guides in every gallery, but visitor surveys showed mixed opinions about the new format.

Which of the following can be inferred from the statements above?

choices.text: The audio guides were universally popular.
The museum changed more than one aspect of the visitor experience.
Attendance rose because the audio guides were cheaper to produce.
Visitor surveys are unreliable in general.
Paper labels always reduce attendance.

distractorRationale.A: Contradicted by the survey's mixed opinions, so it cannot be inferred; what follows is that more than one thing changed at once (B).
distractorRationale.C: The passage never states the guides were cheaper, so this added cause is unsupported; only the multiple-change inference holds (B).
distractorRationale.D: Mixed survey results do not make surveys unreliable in general; that is an overreach beyond the multi-factor inference (B).
distractorRationale.E: Nothing supports a sweeping claim that paper labels always cut attendance; the supportable inference is that multiple aspects changed (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. The museum changed more than one aspect of the visitor experience.

stem:
A retailer believes that extending store hours will increase annual profit.

Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the belief?

choices.text: Whether customers who shop late buy more per visit than daytime customers.
Whether employees prefer shorter shifts.
Whether the retailer uses self-checkout machines.
Whether the store sells seasonal products.
Whether nearby stores have similar hours.

distractorRationale.B: Employee shift preferences bear on morale, not on whether longer hours raise profit; the useful question is whether late shoppers spend more (A).
distractorRationale.C: Whether self-checkout is used does not test the profit claim; evaluating per-visit spending of late customers does (A).
distractorRationale.D: Selling seasonal products is too tangential to assess the profit of extended hours; the key issue is late-shopper spending (A).
distractorRationale.E: What neighboring stores do does not directly test this retailer's profit from staying open later; late-customer spending does (A).

explanation: The correct answer is A. Whether customers who shop late buy more per visit than daytime customers.

stem:
One economist argues that **taxes on sugary drinks unfairly restrict consumer choice**. Another economist replies that **the taxes are justified because they offset public health costs**.

In the argument, the second boldface statement most likely serves as which of the following?

choices.text: A conclusion that directly restates the first boldface statement.
A counterclaim that supports the policy criticized by the first boldface statement.
A definition of consumer choice.
A premise showing that sugary drinks are harmless.
An example of a tax with no economic effects.

distractorRationale.A: The second statement opposes the first rather than restating it, so 'restates' is wrong; it is a counterclaim defending the taxes (B).
distractorRationale.C: It is not a definition of consumer choice; it is a justification of the policy the first statement attacks (B).
distractorRationale.D: It does not claim sugary drinks are harmless; it argues the tax offsets health costs, functioning as a counterclaim (B).
distractorRationale.E: Calling it an example of a tax with no effects misreads it; it asserts a real benefit and serves as a counterclaim (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. A counterclaim that supports the policy criticized by the first boldface statement.

stem:
An article claims that because a neighborhood with more cafes has more art galleries, cafes must cause cultural development.

Which of the following identifies the flaw?

choices.text: It assumes correlation proves causation without considering that both may result from another factor such as neighborhood affluence.
It ignores the possibility that galleries are more common in rural areas.
It compares cafes and galleries that are not on the same street.
It fails to define what counts as cultural development.
It uses too much numerical evidence.

distractorRationale.B: The flaw is not about rural galleries; it is treating a correlation as proof of causation while ignoring a common cause like affluence (A).
distractorRationale.C: Street-level location is not the problem; the reasoning error is inferring causation from correlation (A).
distractorRationale.D: Failing to define cultural development is a quibble, not the central logical error of correlation-to-causation (A).
distractorRationale.E: The argument does not rely on excessive numbers; its flaw is the unjustified causal leap (A).

explanation: The correct answer is A. It assumes correlation proves causation without considering that both may result from another factor such as neighborhood affluence.

stem:
A nonprofit argues that a mentorship program will reduce youth unemployment because participants will gain networks that lead to jobs.

Which of the following is an assumption required by the argument?

choices.text: The mentorship program will enroll a large number of unemployed youth.
Participants who gain networks are more likely to obtain jobs than similar participants who do not.
Most participants will remain in contact with their mentors after the program ends.
Networking is a more effective route to employment than additional job training.
The jobs obtained through the program will pay more than minimum wage.

distractorRationale.A: Enrollment size affects scale but not the logical chain; the argument must assume networks actually raise the odds of getting a job (B).
distractorRationale.C: Staying in contact with mentors is not required for the conclusion; the needed link is that networks improve employment outcomes (B).
distractorRationale.D: The argument never compares networking to job training, so it need not assume networking is superior; it only needs B.
distractorRationale.E: Wage levels are about job quality, not whether participants get jobs at all; the required assumption is B.

explanation: The correct answer is B. Participants who gain networks are more likely to obtain jobs than similar participants who do not.

stem:
A transit authority claims that a new express train will reduce traffic because commuters will switch from cars to rail.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: The train will stop at only three stations.
Most commuters who are likely to use the train already use buses or ride-sharing services rather than driving.
The train line will run every fifteen minutes.
Parking downtown is expensive.
The authority plans a marketing campaign.

distractorRationale.A: Only three stations is a minor limitation and does not directly undercut the car-reduction claim the way showing riders are not drivers does (B).
distractorRationale.C: Frequent fifteen-minute service makes the train more appealing, strengthening the claim rather than weakening it; the weakener is B.
distractorRationale.D: Expensive downtown parking would push people toward the train, supporting the plan; the argument is undermined by riders being former bus or ride-share users (B).
distractorRationale.E: A marketing campaign would help adoption, not hurt the claim; the weakener is that new riders are not coming out of cars (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. Most commuters who are likely to use the train already use buses or ride-sharing services rather than driving.

stem:
A nutrition company says that customers who buy its high-protein snack are more likely to continue exercising regularly.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: Customers who already exercised regularly were the most likely to buy the snack.
Customers who buy the snack are matched with similar customers who do not, and the snack buyers still exercise more regularly.
The snack contains more protein per serving than most competing products.
Most customers who buy the snack say they enjoy its flavor.
Regular exercise has been shown to increase appetite for high-protein foods.

distractorRationale.A: Shows reverse causation or self-selection, which weakens the claim rather than strengthening it; matching similar non-buyers is what supports it (B).
distractorRationale.C: Higher protein content does not establish that the snack sustains exercise habits; the strengthener is the matched comparison (B).
distractorRationale.D: Enjoying the flavor does not link the snack to continued exercise; controlling for similar customers does (B).
distractorRationale.E: This points to exercise causing snack purchases, reversing the intended direction; the strengthener removes that confound via matching (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. Customers who buy the snack are matched with similar customers who do not, and the snack buyers still exercise more regularly.

stem:
A city expanded public Wi-Fi in parks and plazas. In the year after the expansion, reported use of the city app increased, but downloads of the app stayed nearly constant.

Which of the following can be properly inferred?

choices.text: The app became more popular because more people downloaded it.
More existing users likely used the app more often.
The city app was redesigned that year.
The Wi-Fi expansion had no effect on app behavior.
App downloads are a better measure than app use.

distractorRationale.A: Downloads stayed nearly constant, so increased popularity cannot be attributed to more downloads; the inference is that existing users used it more (B).
distractorRationale.C: A redesign is never mentioned, so it cannot be inferred; the supported claim is heavier use by existing users (B).
distractorRationale.D: Rising app use suggests the expansion did have some effect, so 'no effect' is unsupported; the valid inference is B.
distractorRationale.E: Nothing ranks downloads as a better measure; the data point to existing users using the app more often (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. More existing users likely used the app more often.

stem:
A university plans to replace paper textbooks with digital versions and expects the switch to lower costs.

Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the expectation?

choices.text: Whether most students already own a device capable of reading the digital textbooks.
Whether the digital versions require annual licensing fees that exceed the cost of printed books.
Whether digital textbooks can be updated more easily than printed editions.
Whether students retain information as well when reading on screens as on paper.
Whether the printing cost of paper textbooks has been rising in recent years.

distractorRationale.A: Device ownership matters for usability, but the cost expectation hinges on recurring fees versus print costs (B).
distractorRationale.C: Ease of updating is a benefit but does not test whether costs actually drop; comparing licensing fees to print costs does (B).
distractorRationale.D: Information retention is about learning outcomes, not cost; the cost claim is best evaluated by B.
distractorRationale.E: Rising print costs would favor the switch but do not address whether digital fees exceed them; the decisive question is B.

explanation: The correct answer is B. Whether the digital versions require annual licensing fees that exceed the cost of printed books.

stem: What is the main purpose of the passage?

choices.text: To argue that urban beekeeping should be banned in all large cities.
To explain that urban beekeeping can be useful but must be managed carefully.
To show that rooftop honey production is more profitable than rural beekeeping.
To prove that urban insects are healthier than rural insects.
To describe the history of honey production in city centers.

distractorRationale.A: Far too extreme; the passage discusses careful management, not a ban, so the balanced purpose is B.
distractorRationale.C: Profitability versus rural beekeeping is not the passage's focus; its purpose is to weigh benefits against the need for management (B).
distractorRationale.D: The passage does not argue urban insects are healthier; it explains that urban beekeeping is useful but must be managed (B).
distractorRationale.E: A history of city honey production is not the main purpose; the passage's aim is the balanced point in B.

explanation: The correct answer is B. To explain that urban beekeeping can be useful but must be managed carefully.

stem: According to the passage, why do researchers caution against treating all rooftop hives as equivalent?

choices.text: Some rooftops are too windy for bees.
Different neighborhoods offer different levels of forage and seasonal bloom patterns.
City bees produce less honey than rural bees.
Residents prefer native planting initiatives.
Rooftop hives are illegal in most cities.

distractorRationale.A: Wind is not the stated reason; researchers caution because neighborhoods differ in forage and bloom patterns (B).
distractorRationale.C: The honey-yield comparison is not the cited reason for treating hives differently; varying forage and bloom is (B).
distractorRationale.D: Resident planting preferences are not given as the caution; the reason is differences in available forage (B).
distractorRationale.E: Legality is not mentioned as the reason; hives differ because neighborhoods offer different forage and bloom (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. Different neighborhoods offer different levels of forage and seasonal bloom patterns.

stem: The passage suggests that a successful urban beekeeping program is most likely to include which of the following?

choices.text: Only exotic flowers that bloom year-round.
Monitoring colony health and planting native species.
Maximum possible hive density on every rooftop.
Relocating all wild insects outside the city.
Focusing exclusively on honey sales.

distractorRationale.A: Year-round exotic flowers run counter to the passage's emphasis on native species; a successful program monitors colonies and plants natives (B).
distractorRationale.C: Maximum hive density contradicts the call for careful management; the suggested practice is monitoring health and native planting (B).
distractorRationale.D: Relocating wild insects is never proposed; the passage points to colony monitoring and native species (B).
distractorRationale.E: Focusing only on honey sales ignores the management focus; the implied program emphasizes colony health and native planting (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. Monitoring colony health and planting native species.

stem: The author's tone toward urban beekeeping is best described as:

choices.text: dismissive and hostile
balanced and cautious
celebratory and uncritical
sarcastic and playful
indifferent and vague

distractorRationale.A: The author is not hostile; the tone weighs benefits and risks, making it balanced and cautious (B).
distractorRationale.C: It is not uncritical praise; the passage stresses careful management, so the tone is balanced and cautious (B).
distractorRationale.D: There is no sarcasm or playfulness; the measured discussion is balanced and cautious (B).
distractorRationale.E: The author is engaged rather than indifferent; the tone is balanced and cautious (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. balanced and cautious

stem: What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Cold-chain logistics is unnecessary because medicines remain effective at room temperature.
Temperature monitoring has improved visibility, but firms must balance control with practical risk management.
Pharmaceutical companies should ship all biologic drugs by air.
Sensors have eliminated all delivery delays.
Temperature excursions always destroy biologic drugs.

distractorRationale.A: Contradicts the passage, which treats temperature control as important; the main idea balances visibility with practical risk management (B).
distractorRationale.C: Shipping all biologics by air is not advocated; the passage's point is balancing control and risk (B).
distractorRationale.D: The passage does not claim sensors eliminated delays; it argues for balancing control with risk management (B).
distractorRationale.E: Saying excursions always destroy drugs is too absolute and conflicts with the balanced view in B.

explanation: The correct answer is B. Temperature monitoring has improved visibility, but firms must balance control with practical risk management.

stem: According to the passage, what tradeoff can tighter control create?

choices.text: It can increase costs and delay delivery if shipments are held back.
It reduces the need for sensors.
It eliminates all risk in transit.
It makes medicines cheaper to produce.
It guarantees faster warehouse loading.

distractorRationale.B: Tighter control does not reduce the need for sensors; the stated tradeoff is higher costs and delays from holding shipments (A).
distractorRationale.C: Control does not eliminate all transit risk; the passage notes it can raise costs and slow delivery (A).
distractorRationale.D: Tighter control does not make medicines cheaper to produce; it can increase costs and cause delay (A).
distractorRationale.E: It does not guarantee faster loading; the described tradeoff is added cost and delay when shipments are held (A).

explanation: The correct answer is A. It can increase costs and delay delivery if shipments are held back.

stem: Which of the following is implied by the passage?

choices.text: A brief temperature fluctuation is always harmless.
Some shipments may be acceptable even if they experience minor temperature changes.
All biologic drugs have identical storage requirements.
Clinic staff should ignore sensor data.
Risk management is less important than speed.

distractorRationale.A: Too absolute; the passage implies some, not all, fluctuations are tolerable, so 'always harmless' overstates it (B).
distractorRationale.C: Identical storage requirements for all biologics is not implied; what follows is that minor changes may be acceptable (B).
distractorRationale.D: The passage never suggests ignoring sensor data; the implication is that some shipments survive minor excursions (B).
distractorRationale.E: It does not rank speed above risk management; the implied point is that minor temperature changes can be acceptable (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. Some shipments may be acceptable even if they experience minor temperature changes.

stem: The final sentence primarily serves to:

choices.text: introduce a new problem unrelated to logistics.
summarize the author's broader view of the topic.
criticize specific companies by name.
provide an example of a failed shipment.
shift the topic to drug pricing.

distractorRationale.A: The final sentence does not raise an unrelated problem; it sums up the author's broader view (B).
distractorRationale.C: No companies are named or criticized; the closing line summarizes the author's overall stance (B).
distractorRationale.D: It is not a failed-shipment example; it functions as a concluding summary of the author's view (B).
distractorRationale.E: It does not pivot to drug pricing; the sentence rounds off the author's broader perspective (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. summarize the author's broader view of the topic.

stem: What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Digitization makes originals unnecessary.
Digitization changes how archives are studied because choices made in the process shape interpretation.
Scholars prefer digital documents because they are always more accurate.
Only dramatic artifacts should be digitized.
Metadata has no effect on research.

distractorRationale.A: The passage does not claim originals become unnecessary; its main idea is that digitization choices shape interpretation (B).
distractorRationale.C: It does not say digital documents are always more accurate; the point is that process choices affect study (B).
distractorRationale.D: Digitizing only dramatic artifacts is not the main idea; the focus is on how choices shape interpretation (B).
distractorRationale.E: The passage stresses that metadata does affect research, so saying it has no effect contradicts the main idea (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. Digitization changes how archives are studied because choices made in the process shape interpretation.

stem: According to the passage, which aspect of digitization can shape what users notice first?

choices.text: The color of the archive building.
The resolution, metadata, and annotation used.
The age of the archivist.
The number of visitors in the reading room.
The cost of paper storage.

distractorRationale.A: The building's color is irrelevant; what shapes first impressions is resolution, metadata, and annotation (B).
distractorRationale.C: The archivist's age is not a factor; the passage cites resolution, metadata, and annotation (B).
distractorRationale.D: Visitor count in the reading room is not mentioned as shaping attention; technical choices like resolution and metadata are (B).
distractorRationale.E: Paper storage cost is not the stated factor; resolution, metadata, and annotation shape what users notice (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. The resolution, metadata, and annotation used.

stem: The passage suggests that ordinary correspondence may be valuable because it can:

choices.text: become more dramatic when digitized.
reveal more about daily life.
replace all other archival records.
eliminate the need for annotation.
reduce the need for search tools.

distractorRationale.A: Digitization does not make correspondence more dramatic; its value is in revealing daily life (B).
distractorRationale.C: It does not replace other records; ordinary letters are valuable for what they show about everyday life (B).
distractorRationale.D: It does not remove the need for annotation; the cited value is insight into daily life (B).
distractorRationale.E: It does not reduce the need for search tools; the passage values such correspondence for revealing daily life (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. reveal more about daily life.

stem: The author's tone toward digitization is best described as:

choices.text: naive and promotional
critical but constructive
mocking and dismissive
uncertain and confused
enthusiastic and absolute

distractorRationale.A: The author is not naive or promotional; the tone raises concerns while offering guidance, so it is critical but constructive (B).
distractorRationale.C: There is no mockery; the author critiques yet remains constructive (B).
distractorRationale.D: The author is not confused but reasoned; the tone is critical but constructive (B).
distractorRationale.E: The tone is not unqualified enthusiasm; it is critical but constructive (B).

explanation: The correct answer is B. critical but constructive
